clean_story:
Group minds. No single leader.

The word swarm comes from the Old English swearm, meaning a buzzing cloud—like bees lifting as one. But swarms aren’t just insects. They’re a rule: many simple parts acting together, creating patterns no individual could.

A flock of starlings twists like liquid, dodging predators without a commander. Each bird follows three rules: match your neighbor’s speed, steer toward the center, avoid collisions. No bird knows the whole shape—yet the swarm bends as a single thought. Ants build bridges with their bodies; termites construct temperature-controlled skyscrapers taller than humans, using only local cues. Even bacteria swarm: Myxococcus xanthus hunts in packs, secreting enzymes to dissolve prey, then sharing the meal.

Swarming isn’t chaos. It’s decentralized precision. Roboticists now program drone swarms to map disasters or pollinate crops, using the same math as fish schools. The trick? Simple agents + strict local rules = unstoppable adaptability.

Three related subjects are hive intelligence, emergent behavior, and how cities grow.
